definitions.definition: The system that brings oxygen to the body's cells and removes carbon dioxide. It consists of the nose, mouth, trachea, bronchi, lungs, and diaphragm.
The process of taking in oxygen and expelling out carbon dioxide from the body. It is a two-stage process, comprising inhalation and exhalation.
The process of taking in oxygen into the lungs. It is facilitated by the contraction of the diaphragm and the intercostal muscles.
The process of expelling out carbon dioxide from the lungs. It is facilitated by the relaxation of the diaphragm and the intercostal muscles.
A dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. It plays a crucial role in the process of breathing.
A tube that connects the larynx to the bronchi. It is also known as the windpipe.
Two branches of the trachea that lead to the lungs. Each bronchus divides into smaller bronchioles.
Small air passages that branch off from the bronchi. They lead to the alveoli, where gas exchange takes place.
Small air sacs located at the end of the bronchioles. They are responsible for the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide.
The process by which substances move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration.
A colourless, odourless, tasteless gas that is essential for respiration. It is present in the air at a concentration of 21%.
A colourless, odourless gas that is produced as a waste product of cellular respiration.
A group of nerve cells located in the medulla oblongata that control the rate and depth of breathing.
Specialized nerve endings located in the aorta and carotid arteries that detect changes in the concentration of oxygen and carbon dioxide in the blood.
A group of nerve cells located in the pons that help control the rate of breathing.
A group of nerve cells located in the lower part of the pons that help control the rate of breathing.
The maximum amount of air that can be held in the lungs after a maximum inhalation.
The volume of air that is inhaled or exhaled during normal breathing.
The additional volume of air that can be exhaled after a normal exhalation.
The additional volume of air that can be inhaled after a normal inhalation.
The volume of air that remains in the lungs after a normal exhalation.
The maximum volume of air that can be exhaled after a maximum inhalation.
The volume of air that remains in the respiratory passages and does not participate in gas exchange.
